title: How to Choose the Right Matric Subjects for University Admission
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The subject choices you make at the end of Grade 9 are among the most consequential decisions of your school career. The seven subjects you select will determine which university courses you qualify for, which faculties will consider your application, and to a significant extent, what you will study intensively for the next three years. The decision comes at a time when many learners are still discovering their interests and abilities. Here is how to choose your matric subjects strategically so that you keep as many doors open as possible.

How Subject Selection Works in South Africa

The NSC requires you to take seven subjects. Four are compulsory: a home language, a first additional language, Mathematics or Mathematical Literacy, and Life Orientation. The remaining three are elective subjects chosen from a list of approved subjects offered by your school. The compulsory subjects are non-negotiable, but you do have an important choice within them: whether to take Mathematics or Mathematical Literacy. This single decision affects your university options more than any other subject choice.

Mathematics is required for admission to most university courses in the sciences, engineering, health sciences, commerce, and information technology. Mathematical Literacy, while a valuable life skill, does not meet the entry requirements for these courses. If you are considering any career that involves quantitative reasoning, take Mathematics. If you are certain that your career path lies in fields that do not require Mathematics, such as law, social work, or the creative arts, Mathematical Literacy may be sufficient, but check the specific requirements of the courses and universities you are interested in. The safe choice is Mathematics. It keeps more doors open.

Your elective subjects are where you tailor your NSC to your interests and career goals. The choice should be guided by the entry requirements for the university courses you might want to pursue. Each faculty and each course specifies the subjects and achievement levels required for admission. Research these requirements before making your choices. The Universities South Africa website and individual university prospectuses publish the current requirements. Do not rely on what older students or relatives tell you. Requirements can and do change.

Subject Requirements for Popular Faculties

Health Sciences (Medicine, Nursing, Pharmacy, Physiotherapy): Mathematics is required, often at Level 5 or higher. Physical Sciences and Life Sciences are required or strongly recommended. The combination of Mathematics, Physical Sciences, and Life Sciences covers the entry requirements for most health science programs. An A in Mathematics is more valuable than an A in Mathematical Literacy, which does not qualify you at all.

Engineering: Mathematics is required at a high level, typically Level 6 or higher. Physical Sciences is also required. The combination of Mathematics and Physical Sciences is non-negotiable for engineering. Some universities also recommend or require an additional science or technical subject, but Mathematics and Physical Sciences are the essential two.

Commerce (Accounting, Economics, Finance, Business Science): Mathematics is required for most commerce programs, often at Level 5 or higher. Some commerce courses accept Mathematical Literacy, but the more competitive programs require Mathematics. Economics, Accounting, or Business Studies as electives are useful but not always required. Mathematics is the gatekeeper subject for commerce.

Law: Law does not typically require specific subjects beyond the compulsory requirements, but strong performance in languages and humanities subjects strengthens your application. History, Geography, and English are valuable for developing the reading, writing, and analytical skills that legal study demands. Mathematics is not required for most law programs, but a good Mathematics grade demonstrates analytical ability.

Humanities and Social Sciences: These faculties typically have the most flexible entry requirements. Mathematics is usually not required, though some courses like Psychology and Economics may require it. Strong performance in your languages and your chosen elective subjects is sufficient for most humanities programs.

What to Do If You Are Undecided

If you do not yet know what you want to study after matric, choose a subject combination that keeps the maximum number of doors open. Take Mathematics rather than Mathematical Literacy. Take Physical Sciences rather than a less rigorous elective. Include a humanities subject like History or Geography to keep social science and law options available. The combination of Mathematics, Physical Sciences, and a humanities elective is one of the most versatile subject packages available. It qualifies you for science, engineering, health sciences, commerce, and humanities courses.

If you are certain that you do not want a science-based career, a combination of Mathematics, History, Geography, and Business Studies or Economics opens doors to commerce, law, and humanities. This combination is less versatile than the science combination but is well-suited to learners with clear interests outside the sciences.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Do not choose subjects because your friends are choosing them. Your friends are not the ones who will live with the consequences of your choices. Do not choose subjects because you like or dislike a particular teacher. Teachers change. Your subject combination stays on your NSC certificate permanently. Do not choose Mathematical Literacy just because Mathematics is difficult. The difficulty is temporary. The opportunities that Mathematics creates and Mathematical Literacy closes are permanent. Choose based on your career goals and your abilities, not on short-term comfort.

The Bottom Line

Your matric subject choices should be guided by the entry requirements for the university courses you might want to pursue. Take Mathematics rather than Mathematical Literacy unless you are absolutely certain you do not need it. Choose elective subjects that align with your intended faculty and career. If you are undecided, the combination of Mathematics, Physical Sciences, and a humanities elective keeps the most doors open. Research specific requirements for courses and universities before finalizing your choices. The subjects you choose now will shape your options for years to come. Choose strategically.
